title: What's Next — Your Learning Path
subtitle: You've finished the course. Here's where to go from here.

sections.content:
Take a second to look back at where you started. You didn't know what a terminal was. You didn't know what a file path meant, what git does, or how websites actually work.

Now you can navigate any computer from the command line, build and deploy web applications, use AI as a development partner, and follow the same workflow used at every tech company in the world. That's not a small thing. That's a **complete foundation**.

So what comes next? That depends on what excites you. Here are the paths most developers take from where you are now.
Every one of these paths builds on what you've already learned. Pick the one that excites you most — you can always explore the others later.
keyPoints: Web apps (frontend): Learn React, Next.js, Tailwind CSS — build interactive user interfaces
Web apps (backend): Deep-dive Express, PostgreSQL, authentication — build powerful APIs and services
Mobile apps: React Native, Expo — build iOS and Android apps with JavaScript
Automation and scripts: Python, cron jobs, web scraping — automate repetitive tasks
Data and AI: Python, pandas, basic ML — analyze data and build intelligent features

sections.content:
Knowing **what** to learn next matters less than knowing **how** to keep learning. Here's the approach that works:

1. **Build projects** — this is how you learn fastest. Pick something you actually want to exist and build it. You'll learn ten times more from one real project than from ten tutorials.

2. **Use Claude Code as your tutor** — when you're stuck, describe what you're trying to do. Claude can explain concepts, debug your code, and teach you new patterns in the context of your actual project.

3. **Read other people's code on GitHub** — find projects you use and read their source. You learned a code-reading protocol in this course. Use it.

4. **Join communities** — Discord servers, Reddit communities, local meetups. Programming is more fun with other people.

5. **Don't compare yourself to people who've been coding for years** — everyone started where you are. The developers you admire were once confused by the same things that confused you. The only difference is time and practice.

sections.instruction: Your developer journey checklist — everything you've accomplished across this entire course. Check each one and take a moment to feel the weight of what you've learned.

items.text: I can navigate any computer from the terminal
I can read, write, and search files with commands
I can track code history with git
I understand how web applications work
I can build web servers from scratch
I can use AI to accelerate development
I can deploy to the internet

sections.question: You've completed this course. What's the MOST important habit to continue as a developer?
options: Building real projects and learning from the problems you encounter
Reading documentation from start to finish before coding
Only using tools you fully understand
sections.explanation: The best developers learn by building. Real projects create real problems, and solving those problems is how you grow. You don't need to memorize everything — you need to know how to find answers.

sections.prompt: The professional developer workflow follows this cycle: Issue → Branch → Code → Test → Review → Commit → Push → _____ → Merge → Deploy
sections.hint: After pushing your branch, you create this on GitHub to propose merging your changes.
sections.successMessage: Exactly — a Pull Request (PR) is how you propose and discuss changes before merging them into the main branch.

sections.content:
You're not pretending to be a developer. **You are one.** Junior, but real. Every senior developer you meet was once exactly where you are — staring at a terminal for the first time, wondering if they'd ever understand any of this.

You understood it. You built things. You deployed them to the internet.

And with Claude Code as your pair programmer, you can build anything you can describe. You don't need to memorize every API or know every framework. You need to understand the fundamentals — and you do — and know how to ask for help effectively — and you do that too.

The only thing left to do: **go build something.**

completionMessage: You've completed the entire From Zero to Claude Code course. From 'what's a terminal?' to deploying live web applications with a professional workflow. You are a developer. Now go build something amazing.

milestone.title: All Levels Complete!
